title: What Most Newcastle Buyers Miss in Commercial Property Due Diligence
excerpt: Buying commercial property in Newcastle is more complex than buying a house — and the gap between what most buyers check and what they should check is where deals go wrong. Here's what to look at before you sign anything.
content:
# What Most Newcastle Buyers Miss in Commercial Property Due Diligence

Most buyers walk through a commercial property, like what they see, and start negotiating on price. That's understandable — price is visible. The problems are not.

Commercial due diligence is where deals either make sense or fall apart. Spend the time here and you'll either buy with confidence or dodge a bullet. Skip it and you might find out why the vendor was happy to sell.

Here's what actually matters.

## Get the Outgoings Breakdown Before You Do Anything Else

Outgoings are the costs of running the property — council rates, water, building insurance, strata levies if applicable, and sometimes land tax. In a commercial lease, outgoings can be passed to the tenant, absorbed by the landlord, or split. Every lease handles it differently.

Request an outgoings schedule from the agent and cross-check it against the actual lease terms. You want to know exactly what you're on the hook for if there's a vacancy. A property with $40,000 per year in outgoings on a $500,000 purchase is a very different investment to one with $8,000.

This comes up constantly with older stock in suburbs like Hamilton and Mayfield — buildings that look fine from the outside but carry high maintenance and insurance costs that eat into your yield.

## Check the Zoning Before You Fall in Love

Newcastle City Council uses a mix of employment and mixed-use zones — E1, E2, E3, MU1, and others. What those zones permit varies significantly, and it matters a lot depending on how you intend to use or lease the property.

A property zoned E3 Productivity Support might not permit the retail or consulting use you have in mind. A building in MU1 Mixed Use in the Newcastle CBD might allow residential above commercial, which opens different financing and development pathways.

Don't take the agent's word for what's permitted. Pull the planning certificate (s10.7) yourself or have your solicitor do it. It takes five minutes and costs almost nothing. Finding out after exchange that your planned use isn't permitted costs a great deal more.

## Read the Lease, Not Just the Rent

If the property has a tenant, the lease is the asset — not the bricks. The rent figure is what gets advertised. What the lease actually says is what determines whether your bank will lend against it and what the property is genuinely worth.

Four things to look at immediately:

**Weighted average lease expiry (WALE).** If the tenant's lease expires in 18 months with no option, your lender will likely value the property on vacant possession rates, not the tenanted rate. That gap can be significant.

**Rent reviews.** Fixed increases of 3–4% per year are standard in Newcastle commercial leases. CPI-linked reviews give less certainty. Make sure you know which applies and when the next review falls.

**Options.** Does the tenant have the right to renew? Have they exercised it? An unexercised option close to expiry is a risk worth pricing in.

**Make-good obligations.** At lease end, is the tenant required to restore the premises to original condition? If not, and they've fitout the space heavily, that cost may fall on you.

## Don't Assume the Building Is Compliant

Newcastle has a lot of older commercial stock — particularly in the CBD, Wickham, Hamilton, and Broadmeadow. Some of it is excellent. Some of it carries hidden compliance issues.

Three to specifically look for:

**Asbestos.** Buildings constructed before 1990 may contain asbestos-containing materials. You're entitled to request the asbestos register. If there isn't one, treat that as a red flag.

**Fire compliance.** Annual fire safety statements are required for most commercial buildings. Ask for the most recent one and check whether any essential fire safety measures are listed as non-compliant.

**Disability access.** The Disability Discrimination Act applies to commercial premises. If a building has significant access issues and no compliant upgrade path, that can affect your ability to lease it to certain tenants or use it for certain purposes.

A building inspection by a qualified commercial inspector (not a residential inspector) is worth every dollar here. Budget $600–$1,200 for a thorough report on a typical Newcastle commercial property.

## Talk to Someone Before You're Committed

The biggest mistake buyers make is only calling a buyer's agent after they've already found the property, made an enquiry, and started emotionally committing to the deal. At that point it's harder to walk away even when the numbers don't stack up.

Getting independent advice early — before you're attached to a specific property — means you can look at the full picture: the right location for your needs, what comparable sales look like in suburbs like Charlestown, Beresfield, or the CBD fringe, and whether the asking price reflects reality.

If you're thinking about buying commercial property in Newcastle and want a second set of eyes before you go further, we're happy to talk it through.

[Get in touch with the YOS Buyers Agency team →](/contact)
body:
Most buyers do the obvious checks. Title search, building report, lease review. They look at the numbers, they check the zoning, they get a solicitor to review the contract.

And then they miss something that costs them.

Here is what experienced commercial buyers — and the buyers agents who work with them every day — check that most first-timers overlook.

## The outgoings audit

Most buyers check the current outgoings figure. Very few check the trend. Get three years of audited outgoings actuals and look at the trajectory. A building with outgoings rising 8–12% annually is going to erode your yield significantly over a 5-year hold.

Ask specifically about deferred capital works. If the building needs a new roof, a lift upgrade, or a full HVAC replacement — and the outgoings haven't captured it yet — you are about to fund it.

## The lease — all of it, not just the summary

Agents will send you a lease summary. Read the actual lease. Specifically:
- Every special condition (these are where the surprises live)
- The make-good clause in full, not paraphrased
- Assignment and subletting provisions — especially if you plan to sell the property with a tenant in place
- Any side agreements or variations not attached to the main document (ask specifically)

If the agent can't produce a clean copy of every lease document, treat that as a red flag.

## Tenant covenant strength

The yield only exists while the tenant pays. How creditworthy is this tenant? For a private company, you can request last year's financials as a condition of the contract. For a well-known brand, the covenant is public information. For a small local business, you are taking a view on the owner.

Understand what happens to your investment if the tenant doesn't renew. What is the vacancy risk? What would it cost to re-tenant? How long might that take in the current Newcastle market?

## Services condition — not just structural

A building inspection covers the structure. It doesn't always cover the mechanical and electrical services in depth. The HVAC system, the hydraulics, the fire panel, the electrical switchboard — ask specifically whether these have been assessed and when they were last serviced or replaced.

Replacing a commercial air conditioning system can cost $40,000–$150,000. It won't show up in a standard building inspection unless you specifically ask.

## Permitted use vs actual use

Confirm that what the tenant is actually doing in the building is permitted under both the lease and the zoning certificate. Particularly relevant for industrial properties where a tenant may be storing hazardous materials, running a food operation, or operating equipment beyond the permitted use.

If the tenant is in breach of the lease, that becomes your problem the moment you settle.

## Contamination — especially industrial

For any industrial, manufacturing, or service trade property, get a Phase 1 Environmental Site Assessment as part of your due diligence. If there is any indication of contamination — fuel storage, chemical use, automotive work — a Phase 2 investigation is worth the investment before you buy.

Remediation costs can exceed the value of the property. This is not an area to cut corners on.

## What we do

These are the checks our buyers agency team runs on every commercial acquisition. We've seen what gets missed when a buyer goes through the process alone. The cost of proper due diligence is never more than the cost of getting it wrong.

If you're looking at a commercial property in Newcastle or the Hunter and want a second set of eyes on the due diligence — that's what the buyers agency engagement is for.
